narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 46% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for approval. This prediction is based on your academic record, including your Year 1 school decile of 5, which is a bit below the typical student's 5.71, and your Year 3 decile of 5, slightly under the average of 5.53. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 also falls short of the dataset average of 3.21, and your LSAT score of 30.50 is notably lower than the usual 36.70 seen among applicants who tend to pass. Coming from an upper-middle family income bracket, which is around average, and being a white male pursuing full-time studies—both common traits in the dataset—your profile aligns with many students overall. When the model weighed these factors, your Year 3 decile ranking had the strongest influence, nudging the prediction toward a higher risk of bar failure since it was middling compared to stronger performers. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile, which similarly pulled the odds toward failure rather than success. Your family income level contributed next in importance, also leaning slightly against passing, followed closely by your LSAT score, which had a meaningful push toward the failure prediction due to being below par. Your racial background as white was the next factor in line, with a modest influence toward failure risk, and your undergraduate GPA rounded out the top influences in that direction. In contrast, your gender as male was the only notable factor that worked in your favor, gently steering the prediction toward a better chance of passing, while your full-time status had minimal impact. Compared to typical students who get approved, yours stands out as having several core academic metrics in the middle-to-lower range, which together tipped the scales. The good news is you can take actionable steps to boost your odds: retake the LSAT to aim above 36, strengthen your GPA through post-bac courses if possible, and focus on climbing higher in future grade deciles by excelling academically. With targeted improvements in these areas, you'd likely shift into a profile much more like successful bar passers. Keep pushing—you have the potential to turn this around.
